EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulating up to 8 inches was accompanied by blowing snow as a result of northwest winds 20 to 35 mph. The main impact of the storm was to cause difficulties in weekend travel for those who did not delay their travel plans. The storm began on January 31st and continued into the new month through much of February 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches. Accompanying northwest winds gusting to 35 mph caused areas of blowing snow, reducing visibility to 1/4 mile at a few places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late spring storm brought significant snowfall across much of western South Dakota. Rain changed to snow during the day on the 9th, with snow continuing into the next day, moderate to heavy at times. Northwest winds produced areas of blowing and drifting snow, with gusts around 45 mph over the west central South Dakota plains producing blizzard conditions on the 10th. Snowfall ranged from five to ten inches over northwestern South Dakota and eight to 16 inches fell over southwestern South Dakota. The northern Black Hills received 12 to 24 inches.
Down slope winds gusting to 60 mph occurred on the east side of the Sisseton Hills during the morning hours.

Thunderstorms caused damaging winds at numerous locations in southeast South Dakota before sunrise on the morning of June 22nd. Some of the winds spread away from the storms, still at damaging levels despite no longer being directly connected with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 100 mph caused widespread damage to trees and power lines and damaged several homes. Many trees were blown over or completely uprooted. At least one house was completely destroyed, and others had parts of their roofs blown off. Windows were broken and a large camper was rolled several times and torn apart. Other vehicles were damaged, some severely by garage debris or falling trees. Grandstands at a baseball field were damaged. At least one barn and several silos and smaller structures at area farms were destroyed. The elevator in town was severely damaged. No injuries were reported.

A thunderstorm briefly became severe over central Todd County, producing hail to quarter size south of Mission.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe over central Todd County, producing hail to quarter size south of Mission.
A thunderstorm produced penny sized hail south of Wall.

Strong northwest winds, warm temperatures, and low humidity brought on another large fire in Edmunds county. The fire began eight miles south of Craven corner on 363rd avenue just north of Edmunds county road 24. The fire grew quickly and was fought by several fire departments along with the help of local farmers. The fire did threaten a farmyard along 364th avenue. A few FEM electric poles were lost. The fire began in the early afternoon and was contained in the early evening.
An isolated thunderstorm moving into western Stanley county brought quarter size hail.
An isolated thunderstorm moving into western Stanley county brought quarter size hail.

A fire was sparked in Potter county north of Gettysburg by a farmer working the field.  A rock striking a planting disc on an air seeder started it. With very strong northwest winds, warm temperatures, and low humidity, the fire quickly grew and spread southeast. The fire started two miles east and eight miles north of Gettysburg. By the time of the fire was finished, it had burned an area eight miles long by a half-mile wide in some places.  The fire made a run towards the town of Lebanon before being stopped by a slough. A total of 5000 acres were burned.  An abandoned farm house, along with a couple barns, a Quonset building, and semi were all damaged or destroyed by the fire. Highway 212 was closed from Gettysburg to a mile east of Lebanon for three hours due to restricted visibility.  There was a crash on the highway between an RV and semi due to the lowered visibility.  The truck driver suffered minor smoke inhalation. At one time there were 22 fire departments on the scene.

Two rounds of severe weather developed across central South Dakota during the afternoon and late evening hours. The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours of June 19th and quickly produced hail up to two inches in diameter. The thunderstorms proceeded to back build and train over the same areas for the next five hours finally exiting Hand county in the late afternoon.  As a result,  2 to 6 inches of rain had fallen causing flash flooding in northern Hyde and Hand counties. Flooded areas and creeks remained for the next few days. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed late in the evening in western South Dakota transitioning into a widespread wind event as they moved quickly southeast into central South Dakota. During the event, winds from 60 to over 100 mph were measured with Jones, Lyman, and Buffalo counties being the hardest hit. The squall line entered Jones county around midnight and by 1230 am CDT, several semi trucks had been blown over, two machine sheds were destroyed, power poles and lines were snapped, numerous billboards and road signs were damaged, trailers were flipped over, a grain elevator bin's roof was blown off and side caved in, and many trees were downed. Much of the damage occurred in Okaton, Murdo, and Draper and along Interstate-90.||As the storms moved through Lyman County, they damaged several structures,   downed power lines and poles, flipped a pickup truck, along with causing expansive tree damage. In Buffalo county, the storms immediately impacted Fort Thompson, where campers were flipped, structures were damaged, and many trees were downed. Also, in Fort Thompson was the destruction of a trailer house which led to a fatality. The storm then impacted the rest of Buffalo county resulting in the destruction of several grain bins, a hay shed, along with taking the roof off of a hangar near Gann Valley before continuing east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of pasture and agricultural land, several creeks, along with a few roads continued after the earlier heavy rains subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the southern Black Hills, producing hail to golf ball size from Pringle to Buffalo Gap.

The second windstorm in a few days occurred from the evening of June 21st to the morning of June 22nd. Some of the same areas hit by the June 19th and 20th event were impacted once again. The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.

A strong low pressure system moving across the region brought widespread rainfall along with snow to the northwest part of the region. Snow fell across much of north central South Dakota with heavy wet snow occurring west of the Missouri River across Corson and Dewey counties. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Timber Lake; 7 inches 10 miles southwest of McLaughlin; 8 inches 5 miles south of Morristown, and 14 miles northeast of Isabel with 12 inches 5 miles northwest of Firesteel. Travel problems occurred as a result of the heavy snow along with some power outages and tree branches downed. The system also brought some severe weather in the form of large hail to parts of northeast South Dakota.

After a snow storm on May 9-10 left a liquid equivalent of one to three inches of precipitation over southwestern South Dakota and northwestern Nebraska, additional rain combined with the melting snow to cause flooding in the White River, Cheyenne River, and Bad River basins. Record flooding was recorded on the White River in Shannon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred along the White River from the Nebraska state line to Badlands National Park. The White River was over 200 yards wide and at least two feet deep on BIA Highway 41 north of Oglala, where a vehicle was swept off the highway and the passengers had to be rescued. BIA Highway 32 near Slim Butte was washed out and a culvert on BIA Highway 27 washed out and the road was damaged north of Rockyford.

Record flooding was recorded on the White River in Shannon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the South Fork of the Bad River and its tributaries.

The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighty mph winds or higher downed many trees throughout Pierre along with damaging many buildings and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought severe thunderstorms to parts of northeast South Dakota which produced large hail up to the size of tennis balls. The low pressure system also produced heavy rain with heavy wet snow falling across parts of north central South Dakota.

Very heavy rain of over three inches resulted in flash flooding in and around Walker in Corson County. Water was over U.S. Highway 12 for a short period of time disrupting travel.
Runoff from heavy rain on already-saturated ground caused flooding of small creeks and rivers across portions of western South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the south fork of the Bad River and its tributaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain on already-saturated ground caused flooding of small creeks and rivers across portions of western South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along Grizzly Bear Creek from Grizzly Gulch Road through Keystone. Levels on Battle Creek rose close to the train tracks near Judson Camp along Old Hill City Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain on already-saturated ground caused flooding of small creeks and rivers across portions of western South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along Battle Creek upstream of Hermosa to Hermosa, where the river gauge recorded a crest just over 11 feet, three feet over flood stage. Battle Creek flooded a portion of SD Highway 40 west of Hermosa near Paradise Lane and was out of its banks along SD Highway 40 and Playhouse Road. Minor flooding occurred downstream of Hermosa to the Cheyenne River, affecting agricultural land.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along Elk Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain on already-saturated ground caused flooding of small creeks and rivers across portions of western South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused minor flooding along Spring Creek, both upstream and downstream from Sheridan Lake, and along Newton Fork northwest of Hill City. Releases from Pactola Reservoir reached 500 cubic feet per second, and combined with heavy rain, caused minor flooding along Rapid Creek downstream from Pactola Dam to the Cheyenne River. The flooding continued into early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and tracked eastward onto the plains. The storm produced hail to golf ball size and strong wind gusts.
A funnel cloud was produced briefly from a thunderstorm east of Aberdeen.

Two rounds of severe weather developed across central South Dakota during the afternoon and late evening hours. The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours of June 19th and quickly produced hail up to two inches in diameter. The thunderstorms proceeded to back build and train over the same areas for the next five hours finally exiting Hand county in the late afternoon.  As a result,  2 to 6 inches of rain had fallen causing flash flooding in northern Hyde and Hand counties. Flooded areas and creeks remained for the next few days. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed late in the evening in western South Dakota transitioning into a widespread wind event as they moved quickly southeast into central South Dakota. During the event, winds from 60 to over 100 mph were measured with Jones, Lyman, and Buffalo counties being the hardest hit. The squall line entered Jones county around midnight and by 1230 am CDT, several semi trucks had been blown over, two machine sheds were destroyed, power poles and lines were snapped, numerous billboards and road signs were damaged, trailers were flipped over, a grain elevator bin's roof was blown off and side caved in, and many trees were downed. Much of the damage occurred in Okaton, Murdo, and Draper and along Interstate-90.||As the storms moved through Lyman County, they damaged several structures,   downed power lines and poles, flipped a pickup truck, along with causing expansive tree damage. In Buffalo county, the storms immediately impacted Fort Thompson, where campers were flipped, structures were damaged, and many trees were downed. Also, in Fort Thompson was the destruction of a trailer house which led to a fatality. The storm then impacted the rest of Buffalo county resulting in the destruction of several grain bins, a hay shed, along with taking the roof off of a hangar near Gann Valley before continuing east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains of 2 to 6 inches caused flooded creeks, fields, and roads.

The second windstorm in a few days occurred from the evening of June 21st to the morning of June 22nd. Some of the same areas hit by the June 19th and 20th event were impacted once again. The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail caused damaged to siding and vehicles.

The James River at Columbia continued to flood into June with the river rising to above flood stage at Stratford early in the month. The James River went above the flood stage of 14 feet at Stratford on June 5th rising to nearly 15 feet by the end of June.  At Columbia, the river rose to nearly two feet above flood stage on June 3rd to just over 15 feet. Some lowland flooding of roads and agricultural land resulted. The flooding continued at both locations into July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe weather developed across central South Dakota during the afternoon and late evening hours. The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours of June 19th and quickly produced hail up to two inches in diameter. The thunderstorms proceeded to back build and train over the same areas for the next five hours finally exiting Hand county in the late afternoon.  As a result,  2 to 6 inches of rain had fallen causing flash flooding in northern Hyde and Hand counties. Flooded areas and creeks remained for the next few days. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed late in the evening in western South Dakota transitioning into a widespread wind event as they moved quickly southeast into central South Dakota. During the event, winds from 60 to over 100 mph were measured with Jones, Lyman, and Buffalo counties being the hardest hit. The squall line entered Jones county around midnight and by 1230 am CDT, several semi trucks had been blown over, two machine sheds were destroyed, power poles and lines were snapped, numerous billboards and road signs were damaged, trailers were flipped over, a grain elevator bin's roof was blown off and side caved in, and many trees were downed. Much of the damage occurred in Okaton, Murdo, and Draper and along Interstate-90.||As the storms moved through Lyman County, they damaged several structures,   downed power lines and poles, flipped a pickup truck, along with causing expansive tree damage. In Buffalo county, the storms immediately impacted Fort Thompson, where campers were flipped, structures were damaged, and many trees were downed. Also, in Fort Thompson was the destruction of a trailer house which led to a fatality. The storm then impacted the rest of Buffalo county resulting in the destruction of several grain bins, a hay shed, along with taking the roof off of a hangar near Gann Valley before continuing east of the area.

The second windstorm in a few days occurred from the evening of June 21st to the morning of June 22nd. Some of the same areas hit by the June 19th and 20th event were impacted once again. The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail up to 2 inches in diameter damaged siding and crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving supercell thunderstorm developed along the northeastern foothills of the Black Hills, producing large hail and heavy rain in the Piedmont and Black Hawk area. Very large hail was reported around Piedmont, where significant damage was reported. The storm also produced as much as seven inches of rain in less than three hours. Runoff damaged city and county streets and forced the closure of Interstate-90 for several hours.

The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.

Two rounds of severe weather developed across central South Dakota during the afternoon and late evening hours. The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours of June 19th and quickly produced hail up to two inches in diameter. The thunderstorms proceeded to back build and train over the same areas for the next five hours finally exiting Hand county in the late afternoon.  As a result,  2 to 6 inches of rain had fallen causing flash flooding in northern Hyde and Hand counties. Flooded areas and creeks remained for the next few days. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed late in the evening in western South Dakota transitioning into a widespread wind event as they moved quickly southeast into central South Dakota. During the event, winds from 60 to over 100 mph were measured with Jones, Lyman, and Buffalo counties being the hardest hit. The squall line entered Jones county around midnight and by 1230 am CDT, several semi trucks had been blown over, two machine sheds were destroyed, power poles and lines were snapped, numerous billboards and road signs were damaged, trailers were flipped over, a grain elevator bin's roof was blown off and side caved in, and many trees were downed. Much of the damage occurred in Okaton, Murdo, and Draper and along Interstate-90.||As the storms moved through Lyman County, they damaged several structures,   downed power lines and poles, flipped a pickup truck, along with causing expansive tree damage. In Buffalo county, the storms immediately impacted Fort Thompson, where campers were flipped, structures were damaged, and many trees were downed. Also, in Fort Thompson was the destruction of a trailer house which led to a fatality. The storm then impacted the rest of Buffalo county resulting in the destruction of several grain bins, a hay shed, along with taking the roof off of a hangar near Gann Valley before continuing east of the area.

Strong wind gusts accompanied early morning severe thunderstorms across Tripp County.

A slow-moving supercell thunderstorm developed along the northeastern foothills of the Black Hills, producing large hail and heavy rain in the Piedmont and Black Hawk area. Very large hail was reported around Piedmont, where significant damage was reported. The storm also produced as much as seven inches of rain in less than three hours. Runoff damaged city and county streets and forced the closure of Interstate-90 for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Black Hawk when Box Elder Creek overflowed its banks. The flood waters closed the driving lane of eastbound I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second windstorm in a few days occurred from the evening of June 21st to the morning of June 22nd. Some of the same areas hit by the June 19th and 20th event were impacted once again. The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.

Two rounds of severe weather developed across central South Dakota during the afternoon and late evening hours. The first round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours of June 19th and quickly produced hail up to two inches in diameter. The thunderstorms proceeded to back build and train over the same areas for the next five hours finally exiting Hand county in the late afternoon.  As a result,  2 to 6 inches of rain had fallen causing flash flooding in northern Hyde and Hand counties. Flooded areas and creeks remained for the next few days. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed late in the evening in western South Dakota transitioning into a widespread wind event as they moved quickly southeast into central South Dakota. During the event, winds from 60 to over 100 mph were measured with Jones, Lyman, and Buffalo counties being the hardest hit. The squall line entered Jones county around midnight and by 1230 am CDT, several semi trucks had been blown over, two machine sheds were destroyed, power poles and lines were snapped, numerous billboards and road signs were damaged, trailers were flipped over, a grain elevator bin's roof was blown off and side caved in, and many trees were downed. Much of the damage occurred in Okaton, Murdo, and Draper and along Interstate-90.||As the storms moved through Lyman County, they damaged several structures,   downed power lines and poles, flipped a pickup truck, along with causing expansive tree damage. In Buffalo county, the storms immediately impacted Fort Thompson, where campers were flipped, structures were damaged, and many trees were downed. Also, in Fort Thompson was the destruction of a trailer house which led to a fatality. The storm then impacted the rest of Buffalo county resulting in the destruction of several grain bins, a hay shed, along with taking the roof off of a hangar near Gann Valley before continuing east of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second windstorm in a few days occurred from the evening of June 21st to the morning of June 22nd. Some of the same areas hit by the June 19th and 20th event were impacted once again. The first bout came from a bowing line of storms in south central North Dakota that moved southeast and affected parts of northeast South Dakota with damaging winds over 60 mph along with large hail up to baseball size.||The main event occurred later on as severe storms in western South Dakota moved east at around 60 mph and morphed into a severe windstorm.  By early in the morning, the line entered into western Stanley county and produced a wind gust up to 122 mph near Hayes. One person was injured here as a camper rolled. An office building was also moved and grain bins were destroyed. The windstorm continued to move quickly east and reached Pierre and Fort Pierre at around 3 am CDT. Major widespread damage occurred in both cities with winds gusting to over 80 mph.  Countless trees were downed, a mobile home and semi trucks were flipped, a grain silo was destroyed, along with several buildings damaged. Also, power poles were snapped and lines were downed across parts of the area leaving many people without power for several days. Winds as high as 70 mph continued for some time after the storm's passage in Pierre.  The storms then went on to the east and southeast producing more wind damage to structures and trees.

A strong clipper system sliding southeast across the region brought very strong northwest winds in behind it along with scattered snow showers. Snowfall amounts were less than an inch with the snow showers. The very strong northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph with gusts up to 60 mph combined with the existing snow cover and snow showers to bring blizzard conditions to much of northeast South Dakota through the afternoon hours. Travel became difficult, if not, impossible with no travel advised in several counties. There were several cars stranded in Day county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated up to 7 inches along the eastern border of South Dakota from late morning to the evening of January 5th. The snow was accompanied by southeast winds averaging 15 to 30 mph, with some higher gusts, causing areas of blowing snow. Lesser snow accumulations were reported further west in southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches. The snow was accompanied by southeast winds gusting to over 30 mph, reducing visibility to a quarter mile or less in a few areas. The storm forced some schools to close early for the day.

A weak upper level low pressure area moving over the region along with warm and moist air riding over subfreezing air at the surface brought widespread freezing rain in the early and late morning hours.  The freezing rain resulted in ice accumulations of a trace up to a tenth of inch or higher across the region resulting in widespread slippery roads and sidewalks. This resulted in slow travel, several accidents, along with late school starts.

A tight pressure gradient over the Northern Plains produced gusty northwest winds over northwestern South Dakota. The strongest winds were over the higher elevations of Harding County, where sustained winds of 30 to 45 mph and a few gusts of 60 to 65 mph were recorded during the early afternoon.

Heavy rains across the region from a low pressure system brought the James River near Columbia to above flood stage on May 18th. The river went above the flood stage of 13 feet rising to 14.4 feet on May 22nd. Some flooding of agricultural land occurred. The James River continued to flood into June.

Convective showers produced gusty winds across west central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds blew the community center's roof into the building. About 40 people were attending a meeting and three received minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moving across the northern part of the region along with a couple pushes of colder air brought high winds to central and northeast South Dakota on the 11th and 12th.  There were two periods of high winds with this system.  The first one occurred during the afternoon hours of the 11th with the second one occurring during the morning hours of the 12th. Most wind gusts recorded across the region were above 60 mph with several gusts over 70 mph.||In advance of the first cold front, much above normal temperatures occurred on the 11th with highs in the 90s for many locations which brought about several records.  Sisseton tied their all-time record high for October with 95 degrees. The extremely dry conditions, heat, low humidity, combined with the high winds brought extreme fire danger to the region on the 11th. Several fires occurred during the late afternoon and evening of the 11th. The first burst of high winds along a cold front on the 11th lofted a large amount of dust to over 5000 feet as it moved from west to east across the region. The visibility was reduced to a half mile at times in blowing dust across parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Some of the highest wind gusts include: 63 mph at Pierre, 69 mph near Long Lake, 70 mph near Summit, 72 mph north of Vivian, and 75 mph south of Bullhead in Corson county.

Most wind gusts recorded across the region were above 60 mph with several gusts over 70 mph.||In advance of the first cold front, much above normal temperatures occurred on the 11th with highs in the 90s for many locations which brought about several records.  Sisseton tied their all-time record high for October with 95 degrees. The extremely dry conditions, heat, low humidity, combined with the high winds brought extreme fire danger to the region on the 11th. Several fires occurred during the late afternoon and evening of the 11th. The first burst of high winds along a cold front on the 11th lofted a large amount of dust to over 5000 feet as it moved from west to east across the region. The visibility was reduced to a half mile at times in blowing dust across parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Some of the highest wind gusts include: 63 mph at Pierre, 69 mph near Long Lake, 70 mph near Summit, 72 mph north of Vivian, and 75 mph south of Bullhead in Corson county.

A severe thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and tracked eastward across Meade County and southern Ziebach County. The storm produced large hail and wind gusts over 60 mph across the area and a brief tornado southwest of Spearfish.

A severe thunderstorm developed over the northern Black Hills and tracked eastward across Meade County and southern Ziebach County. The storm produced large hail and wind gusts over 60 mph across the area and a brief tornado southwest of Spearfish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds in southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening of August 6th. The storms affected mostly Brookings County, but the first report was some distance away in Gregory County, and there were reports in Moody County also. There were also two tornadoes and one report of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damaged the roof, gutters, and siding of three houses, damaged the roof, patio deck, chimney, and garage of another house, and damaged a fence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down west of Iron Creek Lake. It blew down and snapped aspen, birch, and ponderosa pine trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 8 inches in a few hours caused widespread flash flooding of streets, parks, ditches, homes and businesses, and other low areas in the central and southern parts of Sioux Falls, and just south of the city. Lightning strikes started fires in at least 3 houses during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 8 inches caused widespread flooding of streets, parking lots, parks, ditches, basements and ground floors of homes and businesses, and other low areas. Water several feet deep on roads stalled numerous vehicles. Several vehicles had to be abandoned, some of which became completely submerged. Windows, flooring, and walls in some of the flooded homes and businesses were damaged, causing additional flooding in the affected buildings. Some residents were displaced from their homes by the flooding. The area suffering the worst flooding was in the southwestern corner of Sioux Falls, between 41st and 57th Streets, including the Empire Mall area. The flash flooding included a brief rise in the Big Sioux River at Western Avenue to 3 feet above flood stage.

A surface low pressure area moving across South Dakota combined with a warm front lifting north and a cold front moving east brought many severe thunderstorms to northeast South Dakota. Large hail up to the size of baseballs, damaging winds up to 100 mph, along with a few tornadoes affected much of northeast South Dakota.
A severe thunderstorm tracked northeastward from Crook County, Wyoming across southern Butte County. The storm produced large hail and strong wind gusts in Belle Fourche.

A long-lived complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from southeastern Montana across northwestern South Dakota. The strongest storms produced hail to six inches in diameter, wind gusts over 80 mph, and a tornado in southern Meade County. Significant structure, property, and crop damage was reported in Butte and Meade Counties. Livestock were killed by the large hail in the Nisland area, with one rancher losing 35 sheep. The storms continued into south central South Dakota, but no reports were received due to a widespread power outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball to softball sized hail caused property damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to tennis ball hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from southeastern Montana across northwestern South Dakota. The strongest storms produced hail to six inches in diameter, wind gusts over 80 mph, and a tornado in southern Meade County. Significant structure, property, and crop damage was reported in Butte and Meade Counties. Livestock were killed by the large hail in the Nisland area, with one rancher losing 35 sheep. The storms continued into south central South Dakota, but no reports were received due to a widespread power outage.

Heavy rain over Fall River County and adjacent areas of Nebraska and Wyoming caused widespread flooding across most of southern Fall River County. Hat Creek, Horsehead Creek, and the Cheyenne River downstream of Angostura Reservoir and their tributaries flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Oral occurred after runoff backed up at a plugged culvert along Sand Creek. East Oral Road was underwater, with several homes and businesses in Oral and north of East Oral Road inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Fall River County and adjacent areas of Nebraska and Wyoming caused widespread flooding across most of southern Fall River County. Hat Creek, Horsehead Creek, and the Cheyenne River downstream of Angostura Reservoir and their tributaries flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding was reported along Hat Creek from the South Dakota-Nebraska border to Angostura Reservoir, with the river gauge on Highway 71 measuring a record flow. Several other streams in Fall River County flooded. Indian Creek west of Ardmore was a quarter mile wide with water over the approach on the County Road 5 bridge. Washouts were reported on Hay Canyon Road, North Butte Road, Edgemont Road, and Maitland Road. Horsehead Creek was 200 yards wide north of Oelrichs and covered the northbound lane of US Highway 385 with a foot of water for several hours. Due to the high inflows into Angostura Reservoir, the Bureau of Reclamation increased releases to 18,000 cubic feet per second.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over southeast South Dakota from late morning to the start of the evening on May 10th produced several tornadoes, including one that caused injuries and severe damage in Delmont in Douglas County. The thunderstorms also produced large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding in locations extending to the eastern border of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado, crossing into Douglas County from Charles Mix County, destroyed or damaged 84 structures in and near Delmont, and injured 9 people in Delmont. About 50 of the homes and other structures were destroyed or severely damaged. The damage included numerous small farm buildings totally destroyed, most at four farms south of Delmont, several houses severely damaged, a large and 100 year old church severely damaged, and a newly constructed fire hall destroyed. Power lines and poles were down and the town was without power and water for two days. 100 residents were displaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from southeastern Montana across northwestern South Dakota. The strongest storms produced hail to six inches in diameter, wind gusts over 80 mph, and a tornado in southern Meade County. Significant structure, property, and crop damage was reported in Butte and Meade Counties. Livestock were killed by the large hail in the Nisland area, with one rancher losing 35 sheep. The storms continued into south central South Dakota, but no reports were received due to a widespread power outage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of some roads, including 275th Street where water from flooded ditches ran over the road.

A long-lived complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from southeastern Montana across northwestern South Dakota. The strongest storms produced hail to six inches in diameter, wind gusts over 80 mph, and a tornado in southern Meade County. Significant structure, property, and crop damage was reported in Butte and Meade Counties. Livestock were killed by the large hail in the Nisland area, with one rancher losing 35 sheep. The storms continued into south central South Dakota, but no reports were received due to a widespread power outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several windows of a house were broken and roof damage occurred from wind-driven hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in southern Bowman County, North Dakota and moved south into South Dakota west of US Highway 85. The total path length was almost four miles, and was on the ground over a mile in South Dakota. It caused no damage in Harding County, but did damage buildings on a farm about a mile north of the state line estimated around 100 mph and corresponding to EF1. Additional information on the first portion of this tornado is in the North Dakota, Central and West section of this Storm Data report.

Strong thunderstorms over western and central Pennington County dumped three to four inches of rain during the evening and overnight hours of June 23-24. Runoff from the heavy rain caused areas of flooding along streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding in Rapid Valley. Green Valley Drive was flooded for a quarter of a mile and Anderson Road was impassable. The river gauge near Rapid Valley crested 2.1 feet over flood stage and the river gauge at Farmingdale crested 0.9 feet over flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moving across South Dakota combined with a warm front lifting north and a cold front moving east brought many severe thunderstorms to northeast South Dakota. Large hail up to the size of baseballs, damaging winds up to 100 mph, along with a few tornadoes affected much of northeast South Dakota.

Additional runoff from several heavy rainstorms continued to cause minor to moderate flooding along Spring Creek upstream of and downstream from Sheridan Lake. Releases from Pactola Reservoir reached 500 cubic feet per second, which combined with the heavy rain, caused minor to moderate flooding along Rapid Creek from Pactola Dam to the Cheyenne River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were flooded by heavy rain and high stream levels. On the north side of Rapid City, 143rd Avenue was underwater near Neva Way. In Rapid Valley; Reservoir Road, Green Valley Drive between Reservoir Road and Greenfield Road, South Valley Drive, the east end of Dunn Road, Anderson Road, South Radar Hill Road, and Antelope Creek Road were flooded. Around Box Elder; Elk Vale Road just north of Interstate 90, 155th Street south of Highway 14-16, and the South Ellsworth Road bridge were flooded. Hammerquist Road and 157th Street in the Caputa area were closed. Palmer Gulch Road east of Hill City was underwater. Old Hill City Road and the Needles Highway (SD Highway 87) from Playhouse Road to Sylvan Campground were flooded in various areas. At Hart Ranch, the golf course sustained flooding and debris damaged foot bridges. Spring Creek reached the bottom of the bridge at Mulligan Mile Crossing. High water damaged private bridges in Magic Canyon west of Rapid City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moving across South Dakota combined with a warm front lifting north and a cold front moving east brought many severe thunderstorms to northeast South Dakota. Large hail up to the size of baseballs, damaging winds up to 100 mph, along with a few tornadoes affected much of northeast South Dakota.

A thunderstorm became severe over the northern Black Hills and tracked southeastward across southwestern South Dakota into Nebraska during the night. The storm produced golf ball sized hail and gusty winds, which caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to golf ball size damaged vehicles, trees, and a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe over the northern Black Hills and tracked southeastward across southwestern South Dakota into Nebraska during the night. The storm produced golf ball sized hail and gusty winds, which caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail damaged vehicles and house windows, siding, and roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked southeast from North Dakota across far northwestern South Dakota during the evening. The storm produced large hail, wind gusts to 80 mph, and a tornado in far northern Harding County near the North Dakota border.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked southeast from North Dakota across far northwestern South Dakota during the evening. The storm produced large hail, wind gusts to 80 mph, and a tornado in far northern Harding County near the North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven hail broke windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced four known tornadoes and several reports of large hail in southeast South Dakota during the evening of July 23rd.  The tornadoes caused damage to homes, power poles, and crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado caused an unknown amount of crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced four known tornadoes and several reports of large hail in southeast South Dakota during the evening of July 23rd.  The tornadoes caused damage to homes, power poles, and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damaged homes, mainly roofs and siding. The tornado also damaged a garage, destroyed a partially anchored storage shed, blew down power poles and lines, tossed a 5th wheel camper 75 yards, and damaged crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed near Rockerville and tracked east-southeast to the Hermosa area. The storm produced large amounts of hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph damaged small outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along Rapid Creek and Spring Creek after two days of heavy rain fell on already-saturated soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring Creek covered the entrance road to Hart Ranch. The stream gauge on Rapid Creek above Victoria Creek crested just above flood stage.

A strong clipper low pressure area tracking across northern North Dakota merged with a northeasterly moving surface low pressure area from the Plains. This brought high winds to central and northeast South Dakota from 40 to 50 mph with gusts to over 70 mph. There were some reports of structural damage from the high winds. Some of the highest wind gust include; 67 mph near Clark; 68 mph near Hayes; 71 mph near Reliance, Peever, and Summit along with 77 mph south of Bullhead in Corson county.

A large upper level low pressure area moving slowly across the region brought heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches to most of central and northeast South Dakota.  The snow began on November 30th and continued into December 1st.  Travel was difficult on the roads with travel not advised on many roads. Schools and events were also postponed or cancelled. Some snowfall amounts include; 7 inches at Watertown, Turton, Andover,  Fort Thompson, and Chelsea; 8 inches at Clear Lake, Sisseton, Milbank, Reliance, and  Presho; 9 inches east of Hayes and Kennebec and 10 inches at Murdo.  See December storm data for the end of the storm.

Light to moderate snow affected parts of southeast South Dakota from the afternoon of December 15th to the morning of December 16th. Spotty heavy snow due to thunderstorms was reported during the predawn hours of December 16th in an area from near Sioux Falls north into Brookings County. Strong northwest winds developed and caused some areas of blowing snow after most of the snow had fallen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to locally moderate snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches, including 3.3 inches at Huron. Northwest winds gusting to 40 mph developed after most of the snow fell, causing areas of blowing snow with visibility reduced locally to below one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow affected southeast South Dakota from the night of December 25th into the daylight hours of December 26th. North to northwest winds gusting to 30 mph developed on December 26th and caused areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 7 to 13 inches, including 12.8 inches at Hartford. North to northwest winds gusted to around 30 mph on December 26th, causing areas of blowing snow with visibilities below a half mile in places. The storm affected mainly Christmas weekend travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper low pressure area tracking across northern North Dakota merged with a northeasterly moving surface low pressure area from the Plains. This brought high winds to central and northeast South Dakota from 40 to 50 mph with gusts to over 70 mph. There were some reports of structural damage from the high winds. Some of the highest wind gust include; 67 mph near Clark; 68 mph near Hayes; 71 mph near Reliance, Peever, and Summit along with 77 mph south of Bullhead in Corson county.

A large upper level low pressure area moving slowly across the region brought heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches to most of central and northeast South Dakota.  The snow began on November 30th and continued into December 1st.  Travel was difficult on the roads with travel not advised on many roads. Schools and events were also postponed or cancelled. Some snowfall amounts include; 7 inches at Watertown, Turton, Andover,  Fort Thompson, and Chelsea; 8 inches at Clear Lake, Sisseton, Milbank, Reliance, and  Presho; 9 inches east of Hayes and Kennebec and 10 inches at Murdo.  See December storm data for the end of the storm.

The snow was accompanied by light winds. Several vehicles slid off roads, mainly on the Interstate Highways around Sioux Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 11 inches followed a period of freezing drizzle over southeast South Dakota. The freezing drizzle fell after midnight on November 30th, then the accumulating snow developed from east to west that morning and continued into the morning of December 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, which began on November 30th, wound down on December 1st as a large upper level low pressure area moved slowly across the region. Travel was not advised on many roads and several schools and events were cancelled or postponed. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches occurred across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Specifics include: 7 inches at Watertown, Turton, Andover,  Fort Thompson, and Chelsea; 8 inches at Clear Lake, Sisseton, Milbank, Reliance, and  Presho; 9 inches east of Hayes and Kennebec; and 10 inches at Murdo.

A surface low pressure system that tracked from eastern Colorado to Minnesota brought heavy snow to much of central and parts of northeast, South Dakota. Northwest winds also increased to 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 40 mph causing blowing snow and lowered visibility. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches occurred with this winter storm. A number of schools were delayed or cancelled. Travel became hazardous and was not advised. Snowfall totals include: 6 inches at Pierre, Aberdeen, Onida, and Selby; 7 inches 15 miles northwest of Gettysburg; 8 inches 25 miles west of Onida; 9 inches at Eagle Butte; 10 inches at Murdo and 11 inches 6 miles east of Hayes.

A surface low pressure system that tracked from eastern Colorado to Minnesota brought heavy snow to much of central and parts of northeast, South Dakota. Northwest winds also increased to 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 40 mph causing blowing snow and lowered visibility. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches occurred with this winter storm. A number of schools were delayed or cancelled. Travel became hazardous and was not advised. Snowfall totals include: 6 inches at Pierre, Aberdeen, Onida, and Selby; 7 inches 15 miles northwest of Gettysburg; 8 inches 25 miles west of Onida; 9 inches at Eagle Butte; 10 inches at Murdo and 11 inches 6 miles east of Hayes.

A winter storm tracked across the central and northern plains, bringing snow and gusty winds to western and south central South Dakota. Snowfall across the plains was mostly in the six to 12 inch range, with some higher amounts across the Pine Ridge into south central South Dakota. Across the northern Black Hills, ten to 20 inches of snow were reported. Gusty northwest winds behind the system produced areas of blowing and drifting snow and greatly reduced visibilities at times, especially east of the Black Hills.

Light to moderate snow affected parts of southeast South Dakota from the afternoon of December 15th to the morning of December 16th. Spotty heavy snow due to thunderstorms was reported during the predawn hours of December 16th in an area from near Sioux Falls north into Brookings County. Strong northwest winds developed and caused some areas of blowing snow after most of the snow had fallen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated in varying amounts with heavy snow falling in thunderstorms during the predawn hours of December 16th. The greatest reported accumulation was 9.0 inches 5 miles west northwest of Elkton.

A surface low pressure area which moved across the central plains brought heavy snow along with some blowing snow to much of central and parts of northeast South Dakota.  Heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches along with northwest winds of over 30 mph brought poor road conditions and reduced visibility to the region. Christmas travel was significantly hampered as a result. Some snowfall amounts include: 6 inches at Milbank, Clear Lake, Kennebec and Pierre; 7 inches northwest of Gann Valley; 8 inches at Murdo and Presho; 9 inches east of Hayes, east of Reliance, Miller and Ree Heights and 10 inches southwest of Onida.

Snowfall of 5 to 11 inches followed a period of freezing drizzle over southeast South Dakota. The freezing drizzle fell after midnight on November 30th, then the accumulating snow developed from east to west that morning and continued into the morning of December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle was quickly followed by snow. The snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches over a 24 to 30 hour period that began on November 30th, including 7.0 inches at and near Brookings. The storm resulted in cancellations or cutbacks of school classes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow affected southeast South Dakota from the night of December 25th into the daylight hours of December 26th. North to northwest winds gusting to 30 mph developed on December 26th and caused areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 8 inches, including 8.0 inches at Wessington Springs. North to northwest winds gusted to around 30 mph on December 26th, causing areas of blowing snow with visibilities below a half mile in places. The storm affected mainly Christmas weekend travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow affected southeast South Dakota from the night of December 25th into the daylight hours of December 26th. North to northwest winds gusting to 30 mph developed on December 26th and caused areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches, including 6.0 inches 5 miles south of Chamberlain. North to northwest winds gusted to around 30 mph on December 26th, causing areas of blowing snow with visibilities below a half mile in places. The storm affected mainly Christmas weekend travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passed across the region late on Christmas day into the next morning, producing areas of heavy snow over western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow generally fell from southwestern into south central South Dakota, where five to ten inches was reported. Blowing and drifting snow occurred during the morning hours of the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow affected parts of southeast South Dakota from the afternoon of December 15th to the morning of December 16th. Spotty heavy snow due to thunderstorms was reported during the predawn hours of December 16th in an area from near Sioux Falls north into Brookings County. Strong northwest winds developed and caused some areas of blowing snow after most of the snow had fallen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds, gusting to 40 mph, accompanied light snow which produced little additional accumulation after heavier snow which fell over the eastern edge of the county before the strongest winds. The winds caused areas of blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities to less than a mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, which began on November 30th, wound down on December 1st as a large upper level low pressure area moved slowly across the region. Travel was not advised on many roads and several schools and events were cancelled or postponed. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches occurred across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Specifics include: 7 inches at Watertown, Turton, Andover,  Fort Thompson, and Chelsea; 8 inches at Clear Lake, Sisseton, Milbank, Reliance, and  Presho; 9 inches east of Hayes and Kennebec; and 10 inches at Murdo.
